text: We are presenting the case of a sixty-two year old lady who presented with a symptomatic perineal hernia nineteen months after a laparoscopic abdominal perineal excision. A perineal attempt at repairing the hernia failed six months before this attempt.

We are presenting the case of a sixty-two year old lady who presented with a symptomatic perineal hernia nineteen months after a laparoscopic abdominal perineal excision. A perineal attempt at repairing the hernia failed six months before this attempt. We approached the patient robotically due to the fact that we would have to work deep in the lesser pelvis. Minimal adhesions were divided and after the division we managed to visualize the defect deep in the perineum. The setup of the ports is similar to a robotic lower anterior resection with the patient in Trendelenburg and a slight tilt to the right. The docking of the robot is parallel to the left leg.

The robotic approach is used to access the lesser pelvis, with minimal adhesions being divided to visualize the hernia defect deep in the perineum. The surgical setup includes ports similar to a robotic lower anterior resection, with the patient positioned in Trendelenburg and slightly tilted to the right. The robot is docked parallel to the patient's left leg.

The surgeon lifts the uterus and vagina to reveal the perineal defect below the levator muscles. The posterior part of the vagina is visible. Interrupted sutures are used to perform a levatorplasty, closing the defect with the levator plates. A small gap remains between the vagina and the levatorplasty, which is closed by stitching the posterior vaginal wall onto the levators.

The surgeon lifts the uterus and vagina to reveal the perineal defect below the levator muscles. The posterior part of the vagina is visible. The operation begins with a levatorplasty using interrupted sutures to close the defect with the levator plates. After closing the levator, a small gap remains between the vagina and the levatorplasty, which is closed by stitching the posterior wall of the vagina onto the levators.

The surgeon measures the perineal defect and cuts a Phasix ST mesh to the required dimensions. The mesh is introduced through the assistance port and positioned within the defect. The mesh is marked for orientation and sutured with interrupted sutures at the pelvic brim, the edge of the posterior vagina, and the pelvic sidewalls. The bioabsorbable mesh is placed in direct contact with the small bowel loops to minimize adhesions and fistulization risks.

The surgeon measures the defect for mesh placement and cuts the Phasix ST mesh to the required dimensions. The mesh is introduced through the assistance port and positioned within the defect. The mesh is marked for orientation and is planned to be sutured with interrupted sutures at the pelvic brim, the edge of the posterior vagina, and the pelvic sidewalls on both sides.

The surgeon uses the robotic platform to place interrupted sutures for securing the Phasix ST mesh in the lesser pelvis. The mesh is positioned to avoid a pulsating vessel visible beneath the planned suture site, ensuring safe placement without compromising nearby structures.
The surgeon uses the robotic platform to place interrupted sutures in the lesser pelvis, carefully avoiding a visible pulsating vessel beneath the planned suture site.

The surgeon stitches the Phasix ST mesh at the pelvic sidewalls, pelvic brim, and posterior wall of the vagina to close the gap. After fixation, water is used to activate the bioabsorbable mesh. A CT scan six months postoperatively shows no evidence of small bowel herniation.

The surgeon stitches the Phasix ST mesh at the pelvic sidewalls, pelvic brim, and posterior wall of the vagina to close the gap. After securing the mesh, water is applied to activate it. A CT scan taken six months postoperatively shows no evidence of small bowel herniation.
